content:
# Joshua Chapter 24 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 248-249 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_024_page_001.jpg`

1. And Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and he summoned the elders of Israel, and their heads, and their judges, and their officers; and they presented themselves before God.

2. And Joshua said to all the people: "Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: 'Your fathers dwelt beyond the River from of old — Terah, the father of Abraham and the father of Nahor — and they served other gods.

3. And I took your father Abraham from beyond the River, and I led him through all the land of Canaan, and I multiplied his seed, and I gave him Isaac.

4. And I gave to Isaac, Jacob and Esau. And I gave to Esau Mount Seir to possess it, but Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt.

5. And I sent Moses and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt according to what I did in its midst, and afterward I brought you out.

6. And I brought your fathers out of Egypt, and you came to the sea. And the Egyptians pursued your fathers with chariots and horsemen to the Sea of Reeds.

7. And they cried out to YHWH, and he set darkness between you and the Egyptians, and he brought the sea upon them and covered them. And your eyes saw what I did in Egypt. And you dwelt in the wilderness many days.

8. And I brought you into the land of the Amorites who dwelt on the other side of the Jordan, and they fought with you. And I gave them into your hand, and you possessed their land, and I destroyed them from before you.

9. Then Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab, arose and fought against Israel. And he sent and summoned Balaam the son of Beor to curse you.

10. But I was not willing to listen to Balaam, and he blessed you repeatedly. And I delivered you from his hand.

11. And you crossed over the Jordan and came to Jericho. And the lords of Jericho fought against you — the Amorites, and the Perizzites, and the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Girgashites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites — and I gave them into your hand.

12. And I sent the hornet before you, and it drove them out from before you — the two kings of the Amorites — not by your sword and not by your bow.

13. And I gave you a land for which you did not labor, and cities that you did not build, and you dwell in them. Vineyards and olive groves that you did not plant — you eat from them.'

14. Now therefore fear YHWH and serve him in wholeness and in faithfulness, and put away the gods that your fathers served beyond the River and in Egypt, and serve YHWH.

15. And if it is evil in your eyes to serve YHWH, choose for yourselves today whom you will serve — whether the gods that your fathers served which were beyond the River, or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you are dwelling. But as for me and my house, we will serve YHWH."

16. And the people answered and said: "Far be it from us to forsake YHWH, to serve other gods.

17. For YHWH our God — he is the one who brought us and our fathers up from the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage, and who did these great signs before our eyes, and preserved us in all the way that we went, and among all the peoples through whose midst we passed.

18. And YHWH drove out all the peoples, even the Amorites dwelling in the land, from before us. We also will serve YHWH, for he is our God."

19. And Joshua said to the people: "You are not able to serve YHWH, for he is a holy God. He is a jealous God. He will not forgive your transgressions and your sins.

20. If you forsake YHWH and serve foreign gods, then he will turn and do you harm and consume you, after he has done good to you."

21. And the people said to Joshua: "No, but YHWH we will serve."

22. And Joshua said to the people: "You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen for yourselves YHWH, to serve him." And they said: "We are witnesses."

23. "Now therefore put away the foreign gods that are in your midst, and incline your heart to YHWH, the God of Israel."

24. And the people said to Joshua: "YHWH our God we will serve, and his voice we will obey."

25. And Joshua made a covenant with the people on that day, and he set for them a statute and an ordinance in Shechem.

26. And Joshua wrote these words in the book of the Torah of God. And he took a great stone, and he set it up there under the oak that was in the sanctuary of YHWH.

27. And Joshua said to all the people: "Behold, this stone shall be a witness against us, for it has heard all the words of YHWH that he spoke with us. And it shall be a witness against you, lest you deal falsely with your God."

28. And Joshua sent the people away, each man to his inheritance.

29. And it was after these things that Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of YHWH, died, being one hundred and ten years old.

30. And they buried him in the territory of his inheritance, in Timnath-serah, which is in the hill country of Ephraim, on the north side of Mount Gaash.

31. And Israel served YHWH all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders who prolonged their days after Joshua, and who had known all the work of YHWH that he had done for Israel.

32. And the bones of Joseph, which the Sons of Israel had brought up out of Egypt, they buried in Shechem, in the portion of the field that Jacob had acquired from the sons of Hamor, the father of Shechem, for a hundred qesitah; and they became an inheritance for the sons of Joseph.

33. And Eleazar the son of Aaron died, and they buried him in the hill of Phinehas his son, which had been given to him in the hill country of Ephraim.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **YHWH (יהוה):** Rendered consistently as "YHWH" throughout, preserving the Tetragrammaton as it appears in the Leningrad Codex. This chapter contains 18 occurrences of the divine name, concentrated especially in the historical recital (vv. 2-13) and the covenant dialogue (vv. 14-24).

2. **"Sons of Israel" (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל):** Rendered literally in v. 32 per LOGOS convention rather than the interpretive "Israelites."

3. **"beyond the River" (בְּעֵבֶר הַנָּהָר):** The Hebrew *ever hannahar* in vv. 2, 3, 14, 15 refers to the region beyond the Euphrates (Mesopotamia). Rendered literally rather than paraphrasing as "on the other side of the Euphrates," preserving the Hebrew geographic expression and its thematic weight in the chapter's theology of divine election — YHWH took Abraham *from beyond the River* into the land of promise.

4. **"wholeness" (תָּמִים, tamim):** In v. 14, rendered "wholeness" rather than "sincerity" or "perfection." The Hebrew *tamim* conveys completeness and integrity of devotion — an undivided heart. The LXX Vaticanus reads ἐν εὐθύτητι ("in uprightness"), but the Hebrew points to a fuller concept of covenantal totality.

5. **"holy God" / "jealous God" (אֱלֹהִים קְדשִׁים / אֵל־קַנּוֹא):** In v. 19, *Elohim qedoshim* is morphologically plural ("holy gods") but refers to the one God of Israel — a plural of majesty or intensification. Rendered "a holy God" to preserve monotheistic sense while noting the intensive force. The term *El qannô* ("jealous God") echoes Exodus 20:5 and Deuteronomy 5:9, linking Joshua's covenant warning to the Sinai covenant.

6. **"the oak" (הָאַלָּה, ha'allah):** In v. 26, rendered "the oak" (with the definite article). This is likely the same sacred tree associated with Shechem in Genesis 35:4, where Jacob buried the foreign gods — a deliberate literary echo, since Joshua here commands the people to "put away the foreign gods" (v. 23). Some render *allah* as "terebinth"; the precise species is uncertain, but the definite article suggests a well-known, specific tree.

7. **"qesitah" (קְשִׂיטָה):** In v. 32, this ancient unit of monetary value is transliterated rather than translated, as its exact worth is unknown. It appears also in Genesis 33:19 and Job 42:11. The LXX renders it as "lambs" (ἀμνάδων), suggesting an early tradition that it was a weight shaped like a lamb, but this is uncertain.

8. **"servant of YHWH" (עֶבֶד יהוה):** Applied to Joshua in v. 29 at his death — the same title given to Moses throughout the book (1:1, 1:13, 1:15, etc.). This is the only place in Joshua where the title transfers from Moses to Joshua, forming an inclusio with 1:1 and confirming Joshua's faithful completion of his commission.

9. **Verse 27 — "it has heard":** The stone is said to have "heard" (שָׁמְעָה, shame'ah) all the words of YHWH — a striking personification. The Hebrew is preserved literally rather than softening to "it stands as testimony to." The witness-stone is a known ancient Near Eastern covenant motif (cf. Genesis 31:44-52, the Gilead witness-heap).

10. **"house of bondage" (בֵּית עֲבָדִים):** In v. 17, rendered literally rather than "place of slavery." This is the standard Exodus formula (Exodus 13:3, 14; 20:2; Deuteronomy 5:6) and its preservation here links the people's confession directly to the Decalogue prologue.

11. **Structural note:** The chapter divides into three sections: (a) YHWH's historical recital delivered through Joshua (vv. 1-13), (b) the covenant dialogue between Joshua and the people (vv. 14-28), and (c) the burial notices that close the book (vv. 29-33). The three burials — Joshua, Joseph's bones, and Eleazar — form a literary conclusion to the entire conquest narrative.
